1: The persistent widow parable (Luke 18:1-8) is introduced with its moral before the story: "he told them a parable to show them that they should always pray and not give up." The explicit moral introduction (unusual for Luke) alerts the reader that the parable is analogical rather than allegorical: the judge is not a portrait of God but an extreme contrast that makes the point by kal vachomer (lesser-to-greater). The "always pray and not give up" (proseuchesthai autous dei kai mē enkakein) uses dei (necessity) -- the same divine-necessity word that governs Jesus' own path -- applying it to the community's ongoing prayer.
2: "In a certain town there was a judge who neither feared God nor cared what people thought" -- the two deficiencies define the judge as corrupt. In Jewish ethics, the fear of God was the foundation of impartial justice (Deuteronomy 1:17: "Do not show partiality in judging; hear both small and great alike. Do not be afraid of anyone, for judgment belongs to God"). Caring about human reputation was the social check on a judge's behavior when fear of God failed. A judge without both constraints was the worst possible official: unchecked by either divine accountability or social shame. The Roman provincial court system of the first century included judges (iudices) who could be corrupt and venal, as documented in Cicero's prosecution of Verres.
3: "And there was a widow in that town who kept coming to him with the plea: 'Grant me justice against my adversary.'" -- widows occupied a specific vulnerable position in the ancient legal system. Without a husband or male guardian (kyrios) to represent them, widows had no standing to pursue legal claims in many ancient courts. The OT repeatedly commands justice for widows (Exodus 22:22-24; Deuteronomy 10:18; Luke 24:17; Isaiah 1:17; Jeremiah 7:6; Zechariah 7:10). The widow's repeated coming (ērcheto = imperfect, she kept coming) represents persistent legal pursuit without natural social support. Her adversary (antidikos = legal opponent) held an advantage she could not match with social connections.
4: "For some time he refused. But finally he said to himself: Even though I don't fear God or care what people think..." -- the judge's inner monologue reveals his motivations transparently. The self-characterization as someone who neither fears God nor respects humans (ouk entrepomenos anthropon = not turning in shame before people = shameless) is his own assessment, not the narrator's label. His eventual capitulation is not a moral transformation but a calculation of self-interest: the widow's persistence has become more costly than granting her request.
5: "Yet because this widow keeps bothering me, I will see that she gets justice, so that she won't eventually come and attack me!" -- kopiazein (wearing out/exhausting) in papyri sometimes means physical assault; hina mē eis telos erchomenē hypopiēzē me = "lest finally coming she give me a black eye" (hypopiāzō = to strike under the eye, used in boxing). The judge fears either physical confrontation or, more likely, the exhausting social damage of her persistent public complaint. His self-interest (not justice) is his motive -- which makes the kal vachomer all the more forceful.
6: "Listen to what the unjust judge says" -- Jesus draws the congregation's attention specifically to the judge's own words. The unjust judge (ho kritēs tēs adikias) is explicitly labeled to establish the contrast: the point of the parable depends on the maximum distance between the judge and God. A corrupt, godless, shameless judge who eventually grants justice for self-interested reasons is the least favorable imaginable human authority. If even he responds to persistence, how much more will a just, loving, attentive Father respond to his children?
7: "And will not God bring about justice for his chosen ones, who cry out to him day and night? Will he keep putting them off?" -- the ekdikēsis (justice/vindication/vengeance) of the chosen ones refers to the eschatological vindication of the persecuted community. The term eklektoi (chosen ones) is the LXX and early Jewish term for the covenant community (Isaiah 42:1; Luke 43:20; Luke 65:9, 15, 22; 1 Chronicles 16:13; Psalms 105:43). The question "will he keep putting them off?" (makrothymei ep' autois = is he patient/slow over them?) frames the apparent delay of justice as the real test of faith -- not God's indifference but his patience allowing time for repentance.
8: "I tell you, he will see that they get justice, and quickly. However, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?" -- the parousia question ends the pericope with eschatological urgency: the issue is not whether God will act (he will, quickly: en tachei) but whether the community will maintain persistent faith-prayer until he does. The question "will he find faith?" (ara heurēsei tēn pistin = will he find the faith?) with the definite article (tēn pistin = the faith, the specific faith just described) asks whether this persistent, trusting prayer -- the faith of the widow -- will characterize the end-time community.
9: The parable of the Pharisee and tax collector is introduced with its target audience: "To some who were confident of their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else" (tois pepoithosi eph' heautois hoti eisin dikaioi kai exouthenountas tous loipous = those who trusted in themselves that they were righteous and who treated others with contempt). The pepoithosi (perfect participle of peithō = to trust, convince, have confidence) points to a self-assurance that may have begun as genuine piety but has calcified into self-congratulation. The contrast is the prayer postures of two known social types in the first-century Jewish world.
10: The temple was the appointed location for prayer (1 Kings 8:33-34, 38-39, 42, 48; Daniel 6:10: "three times a day he got down on his knees and prayed, giving thanks to his God, just as he had done before"). The set times of prayer (shacharit = morning, minha = afternoon, arvit = evening) brought worshippers to the temple courts. The Pharisee and the tax collector both go to the temple to pray, establishing them both as Jews engaged in religious observance. The contrast is not religious vs. secular but two different orientations within the same religious practice.
11: The Pharisee "stood and prayed about himself" (stathēis pros heauton tauta prosēucheto = standing, prayed these things to/about himself). The phrase pros heauton is ambiguous: "prayed to himself" (monologue rather than prayer) or "prayed about himself" (content focused on himself). Either reading points to the same issue: the prayer is self-referential, not God-directed. "God, I thank you that I am not like other people -- robbers, evildoers, adulterers -- or even like this tax collector." The gratitude formula (eucharistō soi = I give thanks to you) is appropriate prayer language, but the content expresses comparative righteousness rather than genuine thanksgiving.
12: "I fast twice a week and give a tenth of all I get" -- the Pharisee's claims are accurate and represent genuine piety. Twice-weekly fasting (Monday and Thursday, the market days when most people would see the fasting) exceeded the single biblical mandate (Day of Atonement). Tithing "all I get" (panta hosa ktōmai = everything I acquire) went beyond the biblical requirement (which applied to agricultural produce, Deuteronomy 14:22-23) to include any income. These are not hypocritical claims; the Pharisee actually does these things. The problem is the orientation: these deeds are presented to God as evidence of superiority rather than gratitude.
13: The tax collector "stood at a distance" (makrothen hestōs) -- the opposite of the Pharisee's position of confidence near the temple. The full prayer posture: "would not even look up to heaven, but beat his breast and said, 'God, have mercy on me, a sinner!'" The downward gaze (refusing to look up toward heaven = toward God) and the breast-beating (typtōn to stēthos autou = striking his chest -- the gesture of grief and contrition, cf. Luke 23:48) express the physical embodiment of humility. "God, have mercy on me, a sinner" (ho theos, hilasōtheti moi tō hamartōlō) -- hilasōtheti is the Lxx word for divine propitiation (the cover of the ark in Leviticus 16:15; Psalms 51:1). The prayer asks for God's atoning mercy, not God's recognition of merit.
14: "I tell you that this man, rather than the other, went home justified before God" -- dedikaiōmenos (justified, declared righteous) is the language of the law court: the judge's verdict that the accused person is in right standing. In Pauline theology (Romans 3:24-26; Galatians 2:16-17), this term carries the weight of the Reformation doctrine of justification by faith. Luke's usage here is pre-Pauline: Jesus declares the tax collector justified by God's mercy while the Pharisee is not, because the tax collector approached God on the basis of need and the Pharisee on the basis of merit. "For all those who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted" -- the reversal maxim appears at 14:11 in the context of table manners; here it applies to prayer.
15: People were bringing infants (brephē = newborns or very young children -- a stronger word than paidia = children, used in Mark 10:13; Matthew 19:13) to Jesus for him to touch. The rabbinic tradition of having great teachers touch or bless children is attested: scholars would bring children to outstanding teachers on the Day of Atonement and Sabbath to touch them (b. Kiddushin 32b). The disciples' rebuke (epitimān = to rebuke, the same word used for demon-rebuking) reveals their understanding that Jesus' time and attention were for serious adult matters, not infants.
16: "Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them, for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these" -- the "belonging" of the kingdom to children (tōn gar toioutōn estin hē basileia tou theou = for of such is the kingdom of God) is not about children's innocence (a Romantic notion absent from ancient culture) but about their social status and their receptive posture. Children in the ancient world were legally and socially dependent, without status or claim. The kingdom belongs to those who come as dependent recipients, not as deserving claimants. The immediately preceding parable about the justified tax collector makes this point explicit.
17: "Truly I tell you, anyone who will not receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it" -- the kingdom-reception posture (dēxhtai = receive, accept, welcome) of a child is the model. A child receives a gift without a claim to deserve it; a child acknowledges dependence; a child cannot reciprocate. These are not virtues the child has cultivated but social realities of the child's position. The adult must adopt the posture of the child before the kingdom: receiving rather than earning, dependent rather than self-sufficient. This connects to the tax collector's prayer (Luke 18:13) -- both the child and the tax collector approach as recipients, not deservers.
18: A "certain ruler" (archōn tis) asks about inheriting eternal life. In Jewish expectation, inheriting eternal life (or "the world to come," Heb. olam ha-ba) was the hope of the righteous. The ruler's title archon (ruler, official) may indicate a synagogue official or a member of the local governing council. He addresses Jesus as "Good Teacher" (Didaskale agathe) -- an unusual form of address that Jesus questions: "Why do you call me good?" No one is good except God alone -- a statement that invites the ruler to consider what his address implies about Jesus' identity, or that distinguishes absolute goodness (God's) from derived goodness (human).
19: "Why do you call me good? No one is good -- except God alone" -- the question is not a denial of Jesus' goodness or deity but an invitation to deeper reflection. If Jesus alone is truly good, and goodness belongs to God alone, what does that imply about Jesus? The ruler has addressed Jesus with a title appropriate to God without recognizing what he is saying. The Markan version (Luke 10:18) is identical; Matthew 19:17 modifies slightly to "Why do you ask me about what is good?" -- a textual variant that removes the theological sharpness of the exchange.
20: Jesus cites commandments from the second table of the Decalogue (Exodus 20:12-16; Deuteronomy 5:16-20) -- those governing human relationships rather than the first table's commands about relationship with God. The specific selection (adultery, murder, stealing, false witness, honoring parents) follows Deut 5 order, making the ruler's claim to have kept them from youth ("apo neotes" = from youth/adolescence) plausible: these are the commandments a law-observant Jew would indeed keep. The absence of the commandments against coveting (Deuteronomy 5:21) is significant: the ruler's failure is precisely at the point of covetousness, as Luke 18:22 will reveal.
21: "All these I have kept since I was a boy" (panta tauta ephylaza ek neotes) -- the ruler's claim is sincere, not hypocritical. Paul makes a similar claim about his pre-conversion life (Philippians 3:6: "as for legalistic righteousness, faultless"). Within the framework of Jewish observance, a carefully law-observant person could genuinely claim to have kept the social commandments. The ruler is not lying; he has been a model Jew by the standards of his community. This makes the "one thing lacking" all the more surprising: the problem is not gross sin but a subtle, structural attachment.
22: "Sell everything you have and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me" -- the instruction is unique in Jesus' teaching: he does not give this command universally but specifically to this man at this moment. The instruction identifies the precise point where the ruler's otherwise complete obedience fails: his wealth is his actual security and identity, not God. "Treasure in heaven" (thēsauron en ouranois = heavenly treasury) reflects the Jewish concept of the treasury of merits stored before God (m. Peah Luke 1:1; Tob Luke 4:9: "almsgiving delivers from death and will purge away every sin"). The poverty-redistribution prescription (sell everything, give to the poor) is the specific cure for his specific idolatry.
23: "When he heard this, he became very sad, because he was very wealthy" -- perilypos (very sad/sorrowful) reflects genuine anguish, not mere annoyance. The man is confronted with a real loss. "Very wealthy" (plousios sfodra = extremely/intensely wealthy) establishes that the cost is enormous. Luke's consistent teaching about wealth as spiritual danger (Luke 6:24; Luke 12:16-21; Luke 16:19-31) reaches its clearest confrontation here: the rich man's sadness is the accurate emotional response to discovering that his wealth is the idol that stands between him and eternal life.
24: "How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God!" -- Jesus' comment to the watching crowd reframes the individual encounter as a general principle. Dyskolos (with difficulty/hard) is a comparative: the rich person faces a particular structural obstacle to kingdom entry that the poor do not. The wealth itself is the obstacle: it creates the illusion of self-sufficiency, provides an alternative security, and demands the loyalty that belongs to God. This is not about moral failings of the rich but about the soteriological structure of wealth as a competing trust-object.
25: "Indeed, it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God" -- the camel-through-a-needle's-eye saying is one of the most memorable hyperboles in the Gospels. Various attempts to soften it (the "Needle Gate" theory, or reading kamelos as rope/cable rather than camel) have no early support. The image is deliberately impossible: the largest animal familiar to Palestinian audiences squeezed through the smallest of openings. The hyperbole makes the point forcefully: natural, unaided entry of the wealthy into the kingdom is impossible. Salvation must come from outside the wealthy person's own resources.
26: "Who then can be saved?" -- the disciples' question reflects the common assumption that wealth was a sign of divine blessing and the wealthy were therefore more likely to be saved. In Deut 28-30, covenant obedience was promised to result in prosperity; the wealthy could therefore appear to be the most visibly blessed. If the wealthy cannot easily enter the kingdom, the implied conclusion is that no one can -- the most outwardly blessed face the most difficulty. The question reveals the disciples' underlying prosperity-theology assumption.
27: "What is impossible with man is possible with God" -- Jesus' answer does not soften the statement about wealth but escalates it. Human impossibility (the camel) becomes divine possibility. The salvation of the wealthy is not achieved by effort or generosity but only by divine action that transforms the person's attachment to wealth. Abraham was wealthy (Genesis 13:2); Zacchaeus was wealthy (Luke 19:2-9) and was saved; the principle is not that wealth automatically prevents salvation but that only God can break the hold that wealth has on the human heart.
28: "We have left all we had to follow you" -- Peter's statement parallels the ruler's lack: the disciples had done what the ruler was unwilling to do. "Left all" (aphentes ta idia = leaving our own things) echoes the language of the disciple-calls (Luke 5:11, 28). The implicit question is: what do we receive in return? This is not mercenary calculation but the recognition that radical sacrifice invites a corresponding promise. Peter's statement functions as both a claim and a question: we have done this -- what can we expect?
29: "Truly I tell you, no one who has left home or wife or brothers or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God will fail to receive many times as much in this age" -- the promise of hundredfold return (Mark 10:30: "a hundred times as much in this present age") is reframed by Luke as "many times as much" (pollaplasiōna) without the specific number. The "this age" dimension is significant: the return is not deferred entirely to the future but begins in the present community, where the followers of Jesus receive home, family, and belonging in the new household of the Kingdom. Acts 2:44-45 and Luke 4:32-35 show this communal provision in practice.
30: "And in the age to come, eternal life" -- the two-age framework (this age / the age to come) is standard Second Temple Jewish eschatology (Heb. olam ha-zeh / olam ha-ba), appearing in the Mishnah (m. Avot Luke 2:7; Luke 4:1) and throughout rabbinic literature. "Eternal life" (zōēn aiōnion = life of the age, the life that belongs to the coming age) is the qualitative life of God's kingdom, not merely endless duration. The ruler asked about "inheriting" eternal life (Luke 18:18); Jesus promises it as the ultimate reward of kingdom-oriented renunciation.
31: "Jesus took the Twelve aside and told them, 'We are going up to Jerusalem, and everything that is written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled'" -- the third and most explicit passion prediction in Luke (cf. Luke 9:22, 44). "Everything that is written by the prophets" frames the passion as scriptural fulfillment (cf. Luke 24:25-27, 44-46 where the risen Jesus interprets the suffering as the fulfillment of "Moses and all the Prophets"). The Servant songs of Isaiah (42; 49; 50; Luke 52:13-53:12), Psalm 22, and Zechariah 9-13 are the primary prophetic texts the passion narratives fulfill.
32: "He will be delivered over to the Gentiles. They will mock him, insult him, spit on him, flog him and kill him" -- the specificity of the passion prediction increases with each iteration. The progressive revelations anticipate Luke's passion narrative detail: mockery (Luke 22:63-65), flogging (Luke 23:16, 22), death (Luke 23:46). "Delivered over to the Gentiles" (paradothēsetai tois ethnesin) anticipates the transfer from Jewish authority to Roman authority (Luke 23:1-25), with paradidōmi (hand over/betray) echoing the Judas betrayal throughout the passion.
33: "On the third day he will rise again" -- the third-day resurrection is the consistent element across all three Synoptic passion predictions (Luke 9:22; 9:44 implicit; 18:33 explicit). The "third day" reflects both the OT pattern (Hosea 6:2: "After two days he will revive us; on the third day he will restore us"; Jonah 1:17: three days in the fish; Genesis 22:4: the third day Abraham sees the place of sacrifice) and the fundamental resurrection kerygma (1 Corinthians 15:4: "he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures").
34: "The disciples did not understand any of this. Its meaning was hidden from them, and they did not know what he was saying" -- Luke's threefold statement of incomprehension (ouk synekan, kekrymmenos apo autōn, ouk egindskōn) is the most emphatic in the Synoptics. The passive "was hidden" (kekrymmenos) implies divine concealment -- a motif in the Hebrew prophetic tradition where the full meaning of prophecy is sealed until the moment of fulfillment (Daniel 12:4, 9: "seal up the words"; cf. Luke 24:45: Jesus "opened their minds so they could understand the Scriptures"). The disciples will understand only after the resurrection (Luke 24:8, 44-45).
35: The healing of Bartimaeus (unnamed in Luke, named in Mark 10:46) occurs "as Jesus approached Jericho" -- Luke places the healing before the Zacchaeus encounter in Jericho (Luke 19:1-10), while Mark and Matthew place it as Jesus is leaving Jericho. The differences may reflect different Jericho locations (old Jericho vs. Herodian Jericho are 2 km apart) or different narrative ordering. Old Testament Jericho (Tell es-Sultan) and Herodian Jericho (Wadi Qelt) were distinct sites. Jericho was a wealthy city in Jesus' time, home to Herod's winter palace and a prosperous population.
36: The blind man hears a crowd passing by (ochlou diaporeuomenou) and asks what is happening. His isolation from the crowds is the mark of his marginalization: he cannot see to interpret events; he depends on verbal description. The information that "Jesus of Nazareth is passing by" (Iēsous ho Nazōraios parerchestai) identifies Jesus by his hometown designation -- the standard way to distinguish individuals of the same name (cf. Acts 2:22; Luke 3:6; Luke 4:10; Luke 6:14; Luke 22:8).
37: The crowd's description of Jesus as "Jesus of Nazareth" triggers the blind man's use of a different title: "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!" The title "Son of David" (Huios Dauid) is a messianic designation rooted in the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7:12-16; Psalms 89:3-4, 29-37; Isaiah 9:6-7; Luke 11:1-9; Jeremiah 23:5-6; Ezekiel 34:23-24; Amos 9:11-12). By the first century, "Son of David" was a standard messianic title in Jewish expectation (Psalms of Solomon Luke 17:21-43). The blind man's recognition and declaration of this title demonstrates more insight than the sighted crowds who merely know he is from Nazareth.
38: "He called out: 'Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!'" -- the cry for mercy (eleēson me) uses the same root as the Kyrie eleison prayer and the Lukan parable of the Pharisee and tax collector (Luke 18:13: "God, have mercy on me, a sinner"). The blind man's cry is a prayer addressed to the messianic king, invoking both the Davidic tradition of God's steadfast love (hesed/eleos) and the royal tradition of the king who champions the vulnerable. The insistence despite rebuke (those who led the way rebuked him, Luke 18:39) mirrors the persistent widow of Luke 18:1-8 -- another parable of persistent petition just preceding this narrative.
39: "Those who led the way rebuked him and told him to be quiet, but he shouted all the more" -- the rebuke comes not from Jesus' opponents but from those leading the way for Jesus, presumably disciples or sympathizers. The rebuke (epitiman = rebuke, the same word used for demons and storms) attempts to silence what the rebukers regard as inappropriate or excessive behavior. The blind man's response -- shouting all the more -- is the paradigm of persistent prayer in action, paralleling the parables of the widow and the friend at midnight. His desperation overrides social convention.
40: Jesus stopped and ordered the man to be brought to him -- statheis (standing still) in the middle of the Jerusalem-bound journey. This pause in the most significant journey of Jesus' life (the final approach to Jerusalem) to attend to a single blind beggar enacts the kingdom priority. "Came near, he asked him" (eggisantos de autou epērotosen auton) -- Jesus' approach closes the distance that the blind man's marginalization created. The question ("What do you want me to do for you?") is addressed to the man directly -- giving him agency in his own healing.
41: "Lord, I want to see" (Kyrie, hina anablepsō = Lord, that I may see again) -- anablepō means literally "to look up again," possibly implying he had once had sight. "Lord" (Kyrie) is the resurrection title applied to the earthly Jesus -- Luke's consistent usage in the travel narrative. The simplicity of the request is striking: he does not explain his situation (evident), does not ask for money or position, asks precisely for what he needs. The request receives the same response as the justified tax collector and the returning Samaritan: "your faith has healed/saved you."
42: "Receive your sight; your faith has healed you" (anable pson, hē pistis sou sesōken se) -- the formula hē pistis sou sesōken se appears at Luke 7:50 (the sinful woman), Luke 8:48 (the hemorrhaging woman), Luke 17:19 (the Samaritan leper), and Luke 18:42 (Bartimaeus). The healing/salvation is attributed to faith (trust in Jesus as messianic healer/savior) rather than to Jesus's power alone -- not because the power is not his but because the access to the power comes through faith. The immediate restoration ("immediately he received his sight") continues the Lukan pattern of instantaneous healing that demonstrates the fullness of divine power.
43: Bartimaeus "followed Jesus, praising God. When all the people saw it, they also praised God" -- the twofold praise (Bartimaeus's individual praise and the crowd's corporate response) is the Lukan literary conclusion to a healing narrative. "Praising God" (ainōn ton theon = glorifying/praising God) is the community response to divine action throughout Luke (Luke 2:20; Luke 5:26; Luke 7:16; Luke 13:13; Luke 17:15; Luke 18:43; Luke 23:47; Luke 24:53). The crowd's praise connects individual healing to communal recognition of God's presence in Jesus. Bartimaeus' following of Jesus while praising God frames him as the paradigm disciple: healed, following, praising. He enters Jerusalem with Jesus as one who already confesses him as messianic Son of David.
